quiz_questions.question_text: How is average velocity calculated?

options.text: Total displacement divided by total time
Total mass divided by total volume
Total force divided by total distance
Total energy divided by total time

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity is calculated by dividing the total displacement (change in position) by the total time taken. It tells you the overall rate of position change for the entire trip.

quiz_questions.question_text: Instantaneous velocity describes the velocity of an object at what point?

options.text: Over the course of the entire trip
At one specific moment in time
Only at the very end of the journey
Only at the very start of the trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity at one exact moment in time, not averaged over a period. A speedometer in a car shows instantaneous speed, telling you how fast you are going right now.

quiz_questions.question_text: What tool in a car displays instantaneous speed?

options.text: The fuel gauge on the dashboard panel
The odometer tracking total distance
The speedometer on the dashboard
The rearview mirror above the dash

quiz_questions.explanation: A speedometer displays the instantaneous speed of a vehicle at any given moment. It continuously updates to show how fast the car is moving right now, not an average over the whole trip.

quiz_questions.question_text: When an object moves at constant velocity, how do average and instantaneous velocity compare?

options.text: Average velocity is always much larger
Instantaneous velocity is always larger
They cannot be compared to each other
They are equal to each other at all times

quiz_questions.explanation: When velocity is constant, the velocity at every instant is the same value. Therefore, the average over any time period equals the instantaneous value at every moment. They are identical.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, how do you find instantaneous velocity at a specific time?

options.text: By finding the slope of the tangent line at that point
By measuring the area under the entire graph line
By reading the value directly from the y-axis label
By adding together all the position values shown

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity at a specific time is found by drawing a tangent line to the curve at that point and calculating its slope. The tangent touches the curve at exactly one point.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, average velocity between two points equals what?

options.text: The area between the line and the time axis
The highest position value on the graph line
The slope of the line connecting those two points
The difference in time between those two points

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity between two points on a position-time graph equals the slope of the straight line (called a secant line) connecting those two points. Slope = change in position / change in time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can average velocity be zero even if the object moved during the trip?

options.text: No, if the object moved its average velocity must be positive
Yes, if the object returns to its starting position
No, average velocity always equals instantaneous velocity
Yes, but only if the object never changed direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity equals total displacement divided by time. If an object returns to its starting position, the displacement is zero, making the average velocity zero, even though the object was moving the entire time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which type of velocity tells you how fast an object is moving right now?

options.text: Terminal velocity of the object
Average velocity over the trip
Instantaneous velocity at this moment
Initial velocity at the start

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity tells you the exact speed and direction of an object at a specific moment. It is the velocity 'right now,' unlike average velocity which covers a time interval.

quiz_questions.question_text: Average velocity takes into account which of the following?

options.text: Only the starting velocity of the object
Only the final velocity of the object
The overall displacement and total time
Only the maximum speed reached on the trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity considers the total displacement (final position minus initial position) and the total time elapsed. It does not account for what happened at each individual moment along the way.

quiz_questions.question_text: If an object speeds up and slows down during a trip, its average velocity will most likely differ from what?

options.text: The weight of the object in newtons
Its instantaneous velocity at most moments
The total distance it has traveled overall
The direction in which it is currently facing

quiz_questions.explanation: When an object changes speed during a trip, its instantaneous velocity varies from moment to moment. The average velocity smooths these changes into one value, so it will differ from the instantaneous velocity at most individual moments.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is average velocity?

options.text: The overall displacement divided by the total time elapsed
The velocity an object has at one specific instant in time
The maximum speed an object reaches during any trip
The acceleration of an object divided by its total mass

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity is defined as the total displacement divided by the total time elapsed. It gives a single value that summarizes how position changed overall, regardless of what happened during the trip.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is instantaneous velocity?

options.text: The overall speed for an entire round trip
The force that causes an object to accelerate
The total path length traveled by the object
The velocity of an object at one exact moment

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity at one specific point in time. It captures both speed and direction at that exact moment, like a snapshot of the object's motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tangent line' mean in the context of finding instantaneous velocity?

options.text: A line connecting two distant points on the curve
A line that touches the curve at exactly one point
A horizontal line drawn across the entire graph
A vertical line from the x-axis to the curve

quiz_questions.explanation: A tangent line touches a curve at exactly one point and has the same slope as the curve at that point. Its slope gives the instantaneous velocity at that specific moment in time.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'secant line' mean on a position-time graph?

options.text: A line connecting two points on the curve to find average velocity
A line tangent to the curve showing instantaneous velocity only
A vertical line showing the starting time of the object's trip
A horizontal line showing the object is completely at rest now

quiz_questions.explanation: A secant line is a straight line drawn between two points on a curve. On a position-time graph, the slope of this secant line gives the average velocity between those two points.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'displacement' mean when calculating average velocity?

options.text: The total path length including all turns and detours
The maximum distance from the starting point at any time
The weight of the object multiplied by the total time
The straight-line change in position from start to finish

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement is the straight-line change in position from start to finish, including direction. It differs from distance, which counts the total path. Average velocity uses displacement, not distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'time interval' mean when finding average velocity?

options.text: The exact moment when velocity is measured
The total weight of the object during motion
The duration of time over which motion is measured
The distance between two positions on a map

quiz_questions.explanation: A time interval is the total elapsed time between two moments. When calculating average velocity, you divide the displacement by this time interval to get the overall rate of position change.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'scalar' in the context of speed?

options.text: A quantity that has only magnitude, no direction
A quantity that has both magnitude and direction
A quantity that changes over time continuously
A quantity that can only be measured by graphs

quiz_questions.explanation: A scalar is a quantity described by magnitude (size) alone, with no direction. Speed is a scalar because it tells you how fast but not which way. Velocity is a vector because it includes direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'vector' mean in the context of velocity?

options.text: A quantity that has only magnitude and no direction
A quantity that includes both magnitude and direction
A quantity measured only at a single instant in time
A quantity that describes the mass of the object

quiz_questions.explanation: A vector is a quantity that has both magnitude and direction. Velocity is a vector because it tells you both how fast an object is moving and in which direction it is traveling.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'average speed' mean, and how does it differ from average velocity?

options.text: They are exactly the same quantity with the same definition
Average speed is always smaller than average velocity
Average speed uses total distance; average velocity uses displacement
Average speed includes direction; average velocity does not at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Average speed is total distance divided by total time (a scalar), while average velocity is total displacement divided by total time (a vector). They can differ when an object changes direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'rate of change' mean in the context of velocity?

options.text: The mass of an object divided by its volume
The total energy stored in a moving object
The force required to stop an object quickly
How quickly a quantity changes per unit of time

quiz_questions.explanation: Rate of change describes how quickly a quantity changes over time. Velocity is the rate of change of position, and acceleration is the rate of change of velocity. Both describe change per unit time.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student bikes 600 meters north in 2 minutes, then 600 meters south in 3 minutes. What is the average velocity for the whole trip?

options.text: 0 m/s because the total displacement is zero
4 m/s heading northward for the overall trip
2 m/s heading south for the overall trip
5 m/s heading north for the overall trip

quiz_questions.explanation: The student ends up back at the starting point, so total displacement = 0 m. Average velocity = displacement / time = 0 / 300 s = 0 m/s. Even though the student traveled 1200 m total, the average velocity is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car drives 120 km in 2 hours on a highway. At one point, the speedometer reads 80 km/h. Which value is the average velocity?

options.text: 80 km/h is the average velocity for the trip
60 km/h is the average velocity for the trip
120 km/h is the average velocity for the trip
40 km/h is the average velocity for the trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity = displacement / time = 120 km / 2 h = 60 km/h. The speedometer reading of 80 km/h is the instantaneous speed at that particular moment, not the average for the whole trip.

quiz_questions.question_text: A runner completes a 400-meter circular track in 80 seconds, ending where she started. What is her average velocity?

options.text: 5 meters per second around the track
400 meters per second total distance
80 meters per second for the full lap
0 m/s since she returned to the start

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the runner returns to her starting point, her displacement is zero. Average velocity = displacement / time = 0 / 80 = 0 m/s. Her average speed would be 5 m/s (400/80), but average velocity is zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A taxi speeds up to 50 km/h, slows to 20 km/h in traffic, then speeds back to 50 km/h. At the moment it is going 20 km/h, what type of velocity is 20 km/h?

options.text: The overall average velocity for the trip
The terminal velocity of the vehicle
The instantaneous velocity at that moment
The initial velocity at the start of trip

quiz_questions.explanation: The 20 km/h reading at that specific moment is the instantaneous velocity. It tells you the taxi's speed right then, not the average over the whole trip. Average velocity would require total displacement and total time.

quiz_questions.question_text: A hiker walks 3 km east in 1 hour, rests for 30 minutes, then walks 3 km east in 1 hour. What is the average velocity?

options.text: 2.4 km/h east for the entire trip
6.0 km/h east for the entire trip
3.0 km/h east for the entire trip
0.0 km/h because the hiker rested

quiz_questions.explanation: Total displacement = 3 + 3 = 6 km east. Total time = 1 + 0.5 + 1 = 2.5 hours. Average velocity = 6 km / 2.5 h = 2.4 km/h east. The rest period counts toward total time even though the hiker was not moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball rolls 10 m in the first second and 30 m in the next second. The average velocity over 2 seconds is 20 m/s. Is the instantaneous velocity at 1 second likely 20 m/s?

options.text: Yes, average and instantaneous are always equal
Probably not, since the ball was accelerating and changing speed
Yes, because 20 m/s is the only possible velocity value
No, because instantaneous velocity cannot be calculated ever

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the ball covered different distances each second, it was accelerating. The instantaneous velocity at 1 second was likely between 10 m/s and 30 m/s but not necessarily 20 m/s. Average and instantaneous differ when speed changes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A drone flies 500 m east, then 300 m west, all in 40 seconds. What is its average velocity?

options.text: 20 m/s toward the west direction
8 m/s toward the east direction
12.5 m/s toward the west direction
5 m/s toward the east direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement = 500 m east - 300 m west = 200 m east. Average velocity = 200 m / 40 s = 5 m/s east. The average velocity accounts for direction, unlike average speed which would use total distance (800 m).

quiz_questions.question_text: A train moves at 80 m/s, then brakes to a stop. At the exact moment it is going 40 m/s, a passenger checks the speedometer. What did the speedometer show?

options.text: It showed 80 m/s, the train's initial velocity
It showed 0 m/s, because the train was braking
It showed 40 m/s, the instantaneous speed right then
It showed 60 m/s, the average of 80 and 40 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: A speedometer always shows instantaneous speed. At that exact moment, the train was going 40 m/s, so that is what the speedometer displayed. It does not show averages or initial values.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student walks 100 m to school in 200 seconds, sometimes fast and sometimes slow. Her average velocity is 0.5 m/s. Was she always going 0.5 m/s?

options.text: Yes, average velocity means she was always at that speed
No, she varied her speed so instantaneous velocity changed
Yes, velocity never changes during any walking trip
No, it is impossible to calculate her velocity at all

quiz_questions.explanation: Since she walked fast, slow, then fast, her instantaneous velocity was different at various points. The 0.5 m/s average smooths out these variations. At times she was faster and at times slower than 0.5 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars travel from City A to City B (100 km apart). Car 1 drives at a constant 50 km/h. Car 2 drives 80 km/h then stops for lunch, arriving at the same time. Which is true?

options.text: They have the same average velocity but different instantaneous velocities
They have different average velocities but the same instantaneous velocities
Car 2 has a higher average velocity because it drove faster overall
Car 1 has a higher average velocity because it never stopped anywhere

quiz_questions.explanation: Both cars have the same displacement (100 km) and the same total time, so their average velocities are equal. However, their instantaneous velocities differ: Car 1 was always at 50 km/h, while Car 2 varied between 80 km/h and 0 km/h.
